In the initiation-limited regime as described in the continuum-limit approximation by Erdmann-Pham et al., 2020, the current is mainly determined by the initiation rate and it is given by(12)J=αλ−αλ+(ℓ−1)α.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle J = \alpha \frac{\lambda - \alpha}{\lambda + (\ell -1) \alpha}.$$\end{document}

In our model, we assume that the initiation rate is low compared to elongation and ribosome excluded volume interactions can be neglected. In this case, we can expand the expression of J\begin{document}$J$\end{document} as a function of the ratio α/λ\begin{document}$\alpha/\lambda$\end{document}:(13)J=α{1−ℓαλ+o[(ℓαλ)2]},\begin{document}$$\displaystyle J = \alpha \left \{ 1 - \ell \frac{\alpha}{\lambda}+ o\left[ \left(\ell \frac{\alpha}{\lambda}\right)^{2} \right] \right \},$$\end{document}

to observe that when ℓαλ≪1\begin{document}$\ell\frac{\alpha}{\lambda}\ll 1$\end{document}, or equivalently λ/α≫ℓ\begin{document}$\lambda/\alpha\gg\ell$\end{document}, we can approximate J≈α\begin{document}$J\approx\alpha$\end{document} and(14)ρ≈αλℓif λ/α≫ℓ,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \rho \approx \frac{\alpha}{\lambda}\ell \quad \text{if }\lambda/\alpha \gg \ell,$$\end{document}

an approximation that we will use throughout our model. The initial state probability Θr(N)\begin{document}$\Theta_{r}(N)$\end{document} is the probability of observing N\begin{document}$N$\end{document} ribosomes at the beginning of trace r\begin{document}$r$\end{document}, given the model parameters. We set t=0\begin{document}$t=0$\end{document} when the run-off starts, and we assume that this happens 60 s after HT is added to the medium (Ingolia et al., 2011). At t=0\begin{document}$t=0$\end{document} we can assume that the number of ribosomes N\begin{document}$N$\end{document} on the transcript is given by a Poisson distribution with mean αL/λ\begin{document}$\alpha L/\lambda$\end{document}, as in the absence of inhibitor, and where the low-density approximation in Equation 14 is assumed (Szavits-Nossan and Grima, 2023). Given that trace r\begin{document}$r$\end{document} starts at time tr≥0\begin{document}$t_{r}\geq 0$\end{document}, we assume that the ribosome distribution has not yet been substantially altered by initiation inhibition, and we approximate it by a Poisson distribution with mean corrected by tr\begin{document}$t_{r}$\end{document}(15)pr(N|α,λ)=Poisson(N|α(Lλ−tr)).\begin{document}$$\displaystyle p_{r}(N \,|\, \alpha, \lambda) = \text{Poisson}\left(N \, \Big | \, \alpha \left(\frac{L}{\lambda} - t_{r} \right) \right).$$\end{document}

However, the probability of having N=0\begin{document}$N=0$\end{document} ribosomes on the transcript, hence the probability that a transcript is inactive at the time of HT treatment, may not follow the Poisson distribution – for instance, in the presence of switching between active and inactive states (whether or not the Poisson distribution is still a valid description for N>0\begin{document}$N \gt 0$\end{document} will depend on the switching rates; Szavits-Nossan and Grima, 2023). In practice, we describe the probability for a trace to be inactive as poff\begin{document}$p_{\text{off}}$\end{document}, which is a parameter of the model and it is shared among all traces. Finally, the initial probability vector for trace r\begin{document}$r$\end{document} is given by(16)Θr(N|α,λ,poff)={poffN=0pr(N)/(1−pr(0))N>0.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \Theta_{r}(N \,|\, \alpha, \lambda, p_{\mathrm{off}}) = \begin{cases}p_{\text{off}}&N=0\\ p_{r}(N) \,/\, (1-p_{r}(0))&N \gt 0.\end{cases}$$\end{document}

By choosing such a Poisson distribution, we assume that, in case of bursting, the durations of active and inactive periods are longer than the total mRNA translation time. For the inference, we need to fix the maximum number of ribosomes Nmax\begin{document}$N_{\mathrm{max}}$\end{document} that can be translating the mRNA. We fix Nmax=100\begin{document}$N_{\mathrm{max}}=100$\end{document}, which corresponds to a density ρ≈0.94\begin{document}$\rho\approx 0.94$\end{document} for L=1066,aa\begin{document}$L=1066,{\rm aa}$\end{document} and ℓ=10aa\begin{document}$\ell=10\, \mathrm{aa}$\end{document}.

The transition probability Γ(N|N′,α)\begin{document}$\Gamma(N\,|\,N^{\prime},\alpha)$\end{document} is the probability of jumping from N′\begin{document}$N^{\prime}$\end{document} to N\begin{document}$N$\end{document} in a time dt\begin{document}$dt$\end{document}, given the kinetic parameters α. Because at t≥0\begin{document}$t\geq 0$\end{document} initiation is inhibited, the only possible transitions are those satisfying N≤N′\begin{document}$N\leq N^{\prime}$\end{document}, hence termination events.

When λ/α≫ℓ\begin{document}$\lambda/\alpha\gg\ell$\end{document} the waiting time distribution between successive termination events was derived in Szavits-Nossan and Grima, 2023 and reads(17)Pterm(t)=αλ−αλ−2α(e−αt−e−(λ−α)t).\begin{document}$$\displaystyle P_{\mathrm{term}}(t) = \alpha \frac{\lambda - \alpha}{\lambda-2\alpha}\left(e^{-\alpha t}- e^{-(\lambda-\alpha) t}\right).$$\end{document}

Since we are assuming α≪λ/ℓ<λ\begin{document}$\alpha\ll\lambda/\ell \lt \lambda$\end{document} we can say that λ−αλ−2α≈1\begin{document}$\frac{\lambda-\alpha}{\lambda-2\alpha}\approx 1$\end{document} and e−(λ−α)t≪e−αt\begin{document}$e^{-(\lambda-\alpha)t}\ll e^{-\alpha t}$\end{document}. As a consequence, Equation 17 is approximately equal to the waiting time distribution between initiation events:(18)Pterm(t)≈αe−αt.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle P_{\mathrm{term}}(t) \approx \alpha e^{-\alpha t}.$$\end{document}

So, we can define the transition probability from N\begin{document}$N$\end{document} to N−k\begin{document}$N-k$\end{document} given α as(19)Γ(N−k|N,α)=Poisson(k|αdt).\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \Gamma(N-k \,|\, N, \alpha) = \text{Poisson}(k \,|\, \alpha dt).$$\end{document}

For the inference, we need to set a maximum number of termination events kmax\begin{document}$k_{\mathrm{max}}$\end{document} per time step dt\begin{document}$dt$\end{document}. Considering that the initiation rates measured in the literature with similar systems range from less than 1 ribosome per min up to 5 ribosomes per min (∼0.08 s−1; Boersma et al., 2019; Livingston et al., 2023), we fixed kmax=6\begin{document}$k_{\mathrm{max}}=6$\end{document}, since the probability of 6 initiation events in dt\begin{document}$dt$\end{document} with α=0.08\begin{document}$\alpha=0.08$\end{document} s−1 is about 0.005.

We assume that the emission probability Φ(y|N,t)\begin{document}$\Phi(y\,|\,N,t)$\end{document} – the probability of observing an intensity y\begin{document}$y$\end{document} given N\begin{document}$N$\end{document} ribosomes at time t\begin{document}$t$\end{document} – is lognormal(20)Φ(y|N,t)=1ys(t)2πexp⁡{−[ln⁡y−ln⁡μ(N,t)]22s2(t)}\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \Phi\left(y \,|\, N, t \right) = \frac{1}{y \, s(t)\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp{\left\{ -\frac{\left[ \ln{y}-\ln{\mu(N,t)}\right]^{2}}{2s^{2}(t)}\right\} }$$\end{document}

to account for different sources of noise arising during acquisition and not filtered by the intensity quantification and pre-processing steps (Wildner et al., 2023). These sources include z\begin{document}$z$\end{document}-diffusion of the mRNA, irregular background, fluctuations in laser intensity, photon shot noise, etc. In addition, this choice aims to effectively describe the noise deriving from the uncertainty regarding the ribosome distribution along the mRNA. For a time t\begin{document}$t$\end{document} and N\begin{document}$N$\end{document} ribosomes, an equivalent representation in terms of random variables is(21)Y=μexp⁡(sX)\begin{document}$$\displaystyle Y = \mu \exp\left(sX\right)$$\end{document}

where X\begin{document}$X$\end{document} is a standard normal random variable, μ:=μ(N,t)\begin{document}$\mu:=\mu(N,t)$\end{document} and s:=s(t)\begin{document}$s:=s(t)$\end{document}. In practice, the measured spot intensity y\begin{document}$y$\end{document} is given by the true signal μ multiplied by an exponentially transformed Gaussian noise.

The following discussion is dedicated to the derivation of μ(N,t)\begin{document}$\mu(N,t)$\end{document} and s(t)\begin{document}$s(t)$\end{document} which explains the explicit dependence of the emission probability on time. Notice that the noise parameter s\begin{document}$s$\end{document} can be expressed in terms of the expectation ⟨y(N,t)⟩\begin{document}$\left\langle\,y(N,t)\,\right\rangle$\end{document} and variance σy2(N,t)\begin{document}$\sigma^{2}_{y}(N,t)$\end{document} of y\begin{document}$y$\end{document} as(22)s2(N,t)=ln⁡(1+σy2(N,t)⟨y(N,t)⟩2).\begin{document}$$\displaystyle s^{2}(N, t) = \ln\left(1 + \frac{\sigma^{2}_{y}(N,t)}{\left \langle \, y(N, t) \, \right \rangle^{2}}\right).$$\end{document}

The denoised signal μ is a function of the number of translating ribosomes N\begin{document}$N$\end{document} and time t\begin{document}$t$\end{document} and, since we are neglecting the precise ribosome positions along the mRNA, it is an average over all the possible configurations CN,t\begin{document}$\mathcal{C}_{N,t}$\end{document} of N\begin{document}$N$\end{document} ribosomes along the mRNA at time t\begin{document}$t$\end{document} of run-off. In practice, we consider that the ribosomes are uniformly distributed along the mRNA. We express it as(23)μ(N,t)=⟨I(CN,t)⟩CN,t=b0+γ(t)NiMP,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \mu(N, t) = \left \langle \, I(\mathcal{C}_{N,t}) \, \right \rangle_{\mathcal{C}_{N,t}}= b_{0} + \gamma(t) N i_{\mathrm{MP}},$$\end{document}

where I(CN,t)\begin{document}$I(\mathcal{C}_{N,t})$\end{document} is the intensity generated by the ribosome configuration CN,t\begin{document}$\mathcal{C}_{N,t}$\end{document}, b0\begin{document}$b_{0}$\end{document} is the offset, iMP\begin{document}$i_{\mathrm{MP}}$\end{document} is the intensity of one mature protein and γ is a time-dependent correction factor that accounts for the fact that ribosomes translating the SunTag have intensity smaller than iMP\begin{document}$i_{\mathrm{MP}}$\end{document}. It depends on time because during run-off, the ensemble of possible ribosomes configurations along the mRNA changes, as ribosomes progress on the transcript. Given the length of the SunTag LS\begin{document}$L_{S}$\end{document}, we can express γ(t)\begin{document}$\gamma(t)$\end{document} in terms of the elongation rate λ (see Mean intensity correction factor during run-off for the full derivation):(24)γ(t)=1−(LS−λt)22LS(L−λt)for λt≤LS,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \gamma(t) = 1 - \frac{(L_{S} - \lambda t)^{2}}{2L_{S}(L-\lambda t)}\quad \text{for }\lambda t \leq L_{S},$$\end{document}

which agrees with the result for t=0\begin{document}$t=0$\end{document} previously reported (Aguilera et al., 2019). When all ribosomes have completed the translation of the SunTag, and approximately for λt>LS\begin{document}$\lambda t \gt L_{S}$\end{document}, γ(t)=1\begin{document}$\gamma(t)=1$\end{document}. The value of γ over time agrees well with simulation results over a broad range of ribosome densities (Figure 2C and Figure 2—figure supplement 2A).

We can compute the variance of the total spot intensity in a similar way(25)σ2(N,t):=Var⁡[I(CN,t)]CN,t=ν(t)NiMP2\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \sigma^{2}(N, t) := \operatorname{Var}\left[ I(\mathcal{C}_{N,t}) \right]_{\mathcal{C}_{N,t}}= \nu(t) N i_{\mathrm{MP}}^{2}$$\end{document}

where ν is given by (see Intensity variance for the derivation)(26)ν(t)=1L−λt(L−2LS3−(λt)33LS2)−(1−(LS−λt)22LS(L−λt))2.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \nu(t) = \frac{1}{L - \lambda t}\left(L - \frac{2L_{S}}{3}- \frac{(\lambda t)^{3}}{3L_{S}^{2}}\right) - \left(1 - \frac{(L_{S} - \lambda t)^{2}}{2L_{S}(L-\lambda t)}\right)^{2}.$$\end{document}

The agreement with simulations is good at low densities, while it significantly deteriorates at higher densities (Figure 2D and Figure 2—figure supplement 2A). This is expected as the expressions of both γ(t)\begin{document}$\gamma(t)$\end{document} and ν(t)\begin{document}$\nu(t)$\end{document} are derived under the assumption that the ribosome density is sufficiently low that excluded-volume interactions can be neglected, and the probability to find a ribosome at position i\begin{document}$i$\end{document} can be approximated by N/L\begin{document}$N/L$\end{document}.

We can write the total variance σy2(N,t)\begin{document}$\sigma_{y}^{2}(N,t)$\end{document} of the signal y(N,t)\begin{document}$y(N,t)$\end{document} as the sum of the variance σmeas2\begin{document}$\sigma^{2}_{\text{meas}}$\end{document} due to measurement noise and variance σC2(N,t)\begin{document}$\sigma_{\mathcal{C}}^{2}(N,t)$\end{document} due to the ribosome configurations on the mRNA, and effectively subsume both of them under the log-normal noise. So, the observed intensity y\begin{document}$y$\end{document} is a random variable because of the unknown measurement process and because of the unknown underlying ribosome distribution. Then, the normalized variance in the expression of s\begin{document}$s$\end{document} (Equation 22) reads(27)σy2(N,t)⟨y(N,t)⟩2=σmeas2(N,t)⟨y(N,t)⟩2+σC(N,t)2⟨y(N,t)⟩2\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \frac{\sigma_{y}^{2}(N,t)}{\left \langle \, y(N, t) \, \right \rangle^{2}}= \frac{\sigma^{2}_{\text{meas}}(N,t)}{\left \langle \, y(N, t) \, \right \rangle^{2}}+ \frac{\sigma_{\mathcal{C}}(N,t)^{2}}{\left \langle \, y(N, t) \, \right \rangle^{2}}$$\end{document}

where the average is intended over the measurements and the configurations CN,t\begin{document}$\mathcal{C}_{N,t}$\end{document}. We approximate the first term in the sum as(28)σmeas2(N,t)⟨y(N,t)⟩2≈σmeas2(N,t)⟨I(CN,t)⟩meas2\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \frac{\sigma^{2}_{\text{meas}}(N,t)}{\left \langle \, y(N, t) \, \right \rangle^{2}}\approx \frac{\sigma^{2}_{\text{meas}}(N,t)}{\left \langle \, I(\mathcal{C}_{N,t}) \, \right \rangle_{\text{meas}}^{2}}$$\end{document}

where the average on the right-hand side is performed over the measurements and where we assume that the right-hand side is a constant multiplicative noise that does not depend on the specific ribosome configuration, nor the intensity, and subsumes the uncertainty in the acquisition process. We approximate the second term in the sum as(29)σC2(N,t)⟨y(N,t)⟩2≈σ2(N,t)⟨I(CN,t)⟩CN,t2=σ2(N,t)μ2=1Nν(t)γ2(t)\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \frac{\sigma_{\mathcal{C}}^{2}(N,t)}{\left \langle \, y(N,t) \, \right \rangle^{2}}\approx \frac{\sigma^{2}(N,t)}{\left \langle \, I(\mathcal{C}_{N,t}) \, \right \rangle^{2}_{\mathcal{C}_{N,t}}}= \frac{\sigma^{2}(N,t)}{\mu^{2}}= \frac{1}{N} \frac{\nu(t)}{\gamma^{2}(t)}$$\end{document}

and, by substituting N\begin{document}$N$\end{document} with the average ⟨N⟩=αL/λ\begin{document}$\left\langle\,N\,\right\rangle=\alpha L/\lambda$\end{document},(30)σC2(N,t)⟨y(N,t)⟩2≈λαLν(t)γ2(t).\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \frac{\sigma_{\mathcal{C}}^{2}(N,t)}{\left \langle \, y(N,t) \, \right \rangle^{2}}\approx \frac{\lambda}{\alpha L}\frac{\nu(t)}{\gamma^{2}(t)}.$$\end{document}

Finally, we can use Equation 22 to write the final estimate for s\begin{document}$s$\end{document}(31)s(t)=ln⁡(1+σmeas2⟨I⟩meas2+λαLν(t)γ2(t)),\begin{document}$$\displaystyle s(t) = \sqrt{\ln\left(1 + \frac{\sigma^{2}_{\text{meas}}}{\left \langle I \, \right \rangle_{\text{meas}}^{2}} + \frac{\lambda}{\alpha L}\frac{\nu(t)}{\gamma^{2}(t)}\right)},$$\end{document}

where we have omitted the dependence of I\begin{document}$I$\end{document} on ribosome configurations CN,t\begin{document}$\mathcal{C}_{N,t}$\end{document} for simplicity. Notice that s(t)\begin{document}$s(t)$\end{document} depends solely on time and on the kinetic parameters α and λ. When there are no ribosomes on the transcript or when ν(t)=0\begin{document}$\nu(t)=0$\end{document} (all ribosomes have terminated the translation of the SunTag) the noise is simply given by(32)s0:=ln⁡(1+σmeas2⟨I⟩meas2).\begin{document}$$\displaystyle s_{0} := \sqrt{\ln\left(1 + \frac{\sigma^{2}_{\text{meas}}}{\left \langle I \, \right \rangle_{\text{meas}}^{2}}\right)}.$$\end{document}

Finally, the emission probability is given by(33)Φ(y|N,t)=1ys(t)2πexp⁡{−[ln⁡y−ln⁡μ(N,t)]22s2(t)}\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \Phi\left(y \,|\, N, t\right) = \frac{1}{y s(t)\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp{\left\{ -\frac{\left[ \ln{y}-\ln{\mu(N,t)}\right]^{2}}{2s^{2}(t)}\right \} }$$\end{document}
where μ(N,t)\begin{document}$\mu(N,t)$\end{document} is given by Equation 23 and s(t)\begin{document}$s(t)$\end{document} is given by Equation 31.

For each experiment, we estimate the traces baseline b0\begin{document}$b_{0}$\end{document} in Equation 23 and the term σmeas2/⟨I⟩2\begin{document}$\sigma^{2}_{\text{meas}}/{\left\langle I\,\right\rangle^{2}}$\end{document} in Equation 31 independently of the other model parameters. The offset b0\begin{document}$b_{0}$\end{document} is estimated as the average intensity of the untranslated traces: because it is very consistent across experiments and conditions, we fix it to its average value across experiments, b0\begin{document}$b_{0}$\end{document} = 6 a.u.

The term σmeas2/⟨I⟩2\begin{document}$\sigma^{2}_{\text{meas}}/{\left\langle I\,\right\rangle^{2}}$\end{document} determines the noise parameter s0\begin{document}$s_{0}$\end{document} (Equation 32), which accounts for the measurement noise alone (and not for the uncertainty of the ribosome distribution). It is estimated independently for each experiment (all conditions together) by calculating the variance of untranslated traces σmeas2\begin{document}$\sigma_{\mathrm{meas}}^{2}$\end{document} divided by the mean intensity squared of the trace ⟨I⟩2\begin{document}$\left\langle\,I\,\right\rangle^{2}$\end{document}. The values obtained in this way correspond to s0\begin{document}$s_{0}$\end{document} ranging from 0.31 to 0.38, with mean 0.34 and standard deviation 0.02 – across experiments. In an independent estimate from raw CHX traces described in Preprocessing and data filtering, we obtain s0=0.36\begin{document}$s_{0}=0.36$\end{document}, a value comparable to what was obtained for untranslated traces (Figure 2—figure supplement 1A, B).

sec.title: Mean intensity correction factor at t=0\begin{document}$t=0$\end{document}
sec.p: We compute here the correction factor γ for the total intensity of N\begin{document}$N$\end{document} ribosomes used in Bayesian modeling of run-off traces, at t=0\begin{document}$t=0$\end{document}. The number of epitopes n\begin{document}$n$\end{document} on the SunTag as a function of the position x\begin{document}$x$\end{document} can be approximated as(34)n(x)=nSLS−1(x−1)x≤LS\begin{document}$$\displaystyle n(x) = \frac{n_{S}}{L_{S} - 1}(x-1) \quad x \leq L_{S}$$\end{document}

where nS=24\begin{document}$n_{S}=24$\end{document} is the total number of epitopes encoded in the SunTag and LS\begin{document}$L_{S}$\end{document} is the SunTag length. For x>LS\begin{document}$x \gt L_{S}$\end{document} we have n(x)=nS\begin{document}$n(x)=n_{S}$\end{document}. In the following, we will consider the intensity normalized by iMP\begin{document}$i_{\mathrm{MP}}$\end{document} and use the fraction of epitopes f(x):=n(x)/nS\begin{document}$f(x):=n(x)/n_{S}$\end{document}. We can compute the average intensity for N=1\begin{document}$N=1$\end{document} ribosomes translating the SunTag as(35)μ1=∑x=1Lf(x)p(x)\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \mu_{1} = \sum_{x=1}^{L}f(x)p(x)$$\end{document}

where p(x)\begin{document}$p(x)$\end{document} is the probability of finding a ribosome at x\begin{document}$x$\end{document}. After approximating p(x)=1/L\begin{document}$p(x)=1/L$\end{document} and using the definition of f(x)\begin{document}$f(x)$\end{document} we haveμ1=1L∑x=1Lf(x)=1L[1LS−1∑x=1LS(x−1)+(L−LS)]\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \mu_{1} = \frac{1}{L}\sum_{x=1}^{L}f(x) = \frac{1}{L} \left[\frac{1}{L_{S} - 1}\sum_{x=1}^{L_S}(x-1) + (L-L_{S})\right]$$\end{document}
which yields(36)μ1=1−Ls2L=γ,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \mu_{1} = 1-\frac{L_{s}}{2L}= \gamma,$$\end{document}
where we used∑x=1LS(x−1)=LS(LS−1)2.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \sum_{x=1}^{L_{S}}(x-1)=\frac{L_{S}(L_{S}-1)}{2}.$$\end{document}
For N>1\begin{document}$N \gt 1$\end{document} we have μN=Nμ1=Nγ\begin{document}$\mu_{N}=N\mu_{1}=N\gamma$\end{document}.

sec.title: Mean intensity correction factor during run-off
sec.p: We compute here the correction factor γ for the total intensity of N\begin{document}$N$\end{document} ribosomes used in Bayesian modeling of run-off traces during run-off. While ribosomes run off the transcripts, the proportion of ribosomes on the SunTag changes, which can be taken into account by a time-dependent correction factor γ(t)\begin{document}$\gamma(t)$\end{document}, dependent on the elongation rate λ (that we assume to be uniform along the transcript). We can approximate the occupation probability as(37)p(x|N,t)={0∀x≤xtN/(L−xt)∀x>xt\begin{document}$$\displaystyle p(x \,|\, N, t) = \begin{cases}0&\forall x \leq x_{t}\\ N/(L-x_{t})&\forall x \gt x_{t}\end{cases}$$\end{document}

where we defined xt:=⌊λt⌋∈[1,L]\begin{document}$x_{t}:=\lfloor\lambda t\rfloor\in[1,L]$\end{document}, the average distance covered by the ribosomes in a time t\begin{document}$t$\end{document}. With the same algebra as before, we get μN=Nγ(t)\begin{document}$\mu_{N}=N\gamma(t)$\end{document} with(38)γ(t)=1L−xt(L−LS2−xt(xt−1)2(LS−1))≃1−(LS−λt)22LS(L−λt)∀t≤LS/λ\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \gamma(t) = \frac{1}{L-x_{t}}\left(L - \frac{L_{S}}{2}- \frac{x_{t}(x_{t}-1)}{2(L_{S}-1)}\right) \simeq 1 - \frac{(L_{S} - \lambda t)^{2}}{2L_{S}(L-\lambda t)}\quad \forall t \leq L_{S} / \lambda$$\end{document}
else γ(t)=1\begin{document}$\gamma(t)=1$\end{document}.

sec.title: Intensity variance
sec.p: We compute the variance V1(y)\begin{document}$V_{1}(y)$\end{document} of the intensity y\begin{document}$y$\end{document} produced by N=1\begin{document}$N=1$\end{document} ribosomes at time t\begin{document}$t$\end{document} during run-off, in units of iMP2\begin{document}$i_{\mathrm{MP}}^{2}$\end{document} (t=0\begin{document}$t=0$\end{document} corresponds to when run-off starts). The variance for N\begin{document}$N$\end{document} ribosomes will be the sum of the variances: VN(y)=NV1(y)\begin{document}$V_{N}(y)=NV_{1}(y)$\end{document}. We can write the variance as(39)V1(y)=⟨y2⟩−μ12\begin{document}$$\displaystyle V_{1}(y) = \left \langle \, y^2 \, \right \rangle- \mu_{1}^{2}$$\end{document}

with μ1\begin{document}$\mu_{1}$\end{document} given by Equation 36 and(40)⟨y2⟩=∑x=xtLf(x)2p(x)=1L−xt(∑x=1Lf(x)2−∑x=1xtf(x)2)\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \left \langle \, y^2 \, \right \rangle= \sum_{x=x_t}^{L}f(x)^{2} p(x) = \frac{1}{L-x_{t}}\left(\sum_{x=1}^{L}f(x)^{2} - \sum_{x=1}^{x_t}f(x)^{2} \right)$$\end{document}

where xt:=⌊λt⌋∈[1,L]\begin{document}$x_{t}:=\lfloor\lambda t\rfloor\in[1,L]$\end{document} as before. The first summation gives∑x=1Lf(x)2=1(LS−1)2∑x=1LS(x−1)2+L−LS\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \sum_{x=1}^{L}f(x)^{2}&= \frac{1}{(L_{S} - 1)^{2}}\sum_{x=1}^{L_S}(x-1)^{2} + L-L_{S}$$\end{document}=1(LS−1)2(LS(LS+1)(2LS+1)6−LS(LS+1)+LS)+L−LS\begin{document}$$\displaystyle &= \frac{1}{(L_{S} - 1)^{2}}\left(\frac{L_{S}(L_{S}+1)(2L_{S}+1)}{6}- L_{S}(L_{S}+1)+L_{S}\right) + L-L_{S}$$\end{document}=LS(2LS−1)6(LS−1)+L−LS≈L−2LS3.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle &= \frac{L_{S}(2L_{S}-1)}{6(L_{S}-1)}+ L - L_{S} \approx L - \frac{2L_{S}}{3}.$$\end{document}

The second term∑x=1xtf(x)2=1(LS−1)2∑x=1xt(x−1)2\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \sum_{x=1}^{x_t}f(x)^{2}&= \frac{1}{(L_{S} - 1)^{2}}\sum_{x=1}^{x_t}(x-1)^{2}$$\end{document}=1(LS−1)2(xt(xt+1)(2xt+1)6−xt(xt+1)+xt)\begin{document}$$\displaystyle &= \frac{1}{(L_{S}-1)^{2}}\left(\frac{x_{t}(x_{t}+1)(2x_{t}+1)}{6}- x_{t}(x_{t}+1)+x_{t}\right)$$\end{document}=xt(2xt−1)(xt−1)6(LS−1)2≈(λt)33LS2.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle &= \frac{x_{t}(2x_{t}-1)(x_{t}-1)}{6(L_{S}-1)^{2}}\approx \frac{(\lambda t)^{3}}{3L_{S}^{2}}.$$\end{document}

Finally,⟨y2⟩≈1L−λt(L−2LS3−(λt)33LS2).\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \left \langle \, y^2 \, \right \rangle\approx \frac{1}{L - \lambda t}\left(L - \frac{2L_{S}}{3}- \frac{(\lambda t)^{3}}{3L_{S}^{2}}\right).$$\end{document}

The final results are then(41)V1(y)=1L−λt(L−2LS3−(λt)33LS2)−(1−(LS−λt)22LS(L−λt))2.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle V_{1}(y) = \frac{1}{L - \lambda t}\left(L - \frac{2L_{S}}{3}- \frac{(\lambda t)^{3}}{3L_{S}^{2}}\right) - \left(1 - \frac{(L_{S} - \lambda t)^{2}}{2L_{S}(L-\lambda t)}\right)^{2}.$$\end{document}

sec.title: Simulation of SunTag traces
sec.p: SunTag traces are obtained in silico by simulating the homogeneous ℓ\begin{document}$\ell$\end{document}-TASEP with the Gillespie algorithm (Gillespie, 1976). The algorithm is formulated for a general set of site-specific elongation rates λi\begin{document}$\lambda_{i}$\end{document}, but for the purposes of this study, we simulate a homogeneous system with all elongation rates equal to λ; for simplicity, the termination rate λL−1\begin{document}$\lambda_{L-1}$\end{document} is considered equal to the elongation rate. We first describe the algorithm used for the ℓ\begin{document}$\ell$\end{document}-TASEP simulations without bursting, and then we describe its generalization for a bursting initiation.

The simulation starts at t=0\begin{document}$t=0$\end{document}. At any step of the simulation, a vector τ=(τ0,…,τL−1)∈{0,1}L\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\tau}=(\tau_{0},\dots,\tau_{L-1})\in\{0,1\}^{L}$\end{document} specifies the ribosome occupancy state of the mRNA (of length L\begin{document}$L$\end{document}) and a list Ω\begin{document}$\Omega$\end{document} specifies all the possible reactions and their associated time. In particular, ωk∈Ω\begin{document}$\omega_{k}\in\Omega$\end{document} is a list of two elements: ωk[0]∈{0,…,L−1}\begin{document}$\omega_{k}[0]\in\{0,\dots,L-1\}$\end{document} specifies the event and ωk[1]∈R+\begin{document}$\omega_{k}[1]\in\mathbb{R}^{+}$\end{document} its time of occurrence. ωk[0]=−1\begin{document}$\omega_{k}[0]=-1$\end{document} corresponds to an initiation event, ωk[0]=L−1\begin{document}$\omega_{k}[0]=L-1$\end{document} a termination event, and ωk[0]∈{0,…,L−2}\begin{document}$\omega_{k}[0]\in\{0,\dots,L-2\}$\end{document} an elongation event at position ωk[0]\begin{document}$\omega_{k}[0]$\end{document}. The list Ω\begin{document}$\Omega$\end{document} is kept ordered according to the time of occurrence, such that the first element of the list is the one occurring earlier in time and ωk[1]≤ωk+1[1]\begin{document}$\omega_{k}[1]\leq\omega_{k+1}[1]$\end{document}. In the following Exp⁡(λ)\begin{document}$\operatorname{Exp}(\lambda)$\end{document} is the exponential distribution with scale parameter λ.

list-item.p: Remove the event ω1\begin{document}$\omega_{1}$\end{document} from Ω\begin{document}$\Omega$\end{document} (let i=ω1[0]\begin{document}$i=\omega_{1}[0]$\end{document} and dt=ω1[1]\begin{document}$dt=\omega_{1}[1]$\end{document}).

Update the total time t←t+dt\begin{document}$t\leftarrow t+dt$\end{document} and the single events time ωk[1]←ωk[1]−dt∀k.\begin{document}$\omega_{k}[1]\leftarrow\omega_{k}[1]-dt\;\forall k.$\end{document}
Find possible new reactions and sample their associated times:

If i=L−1\begin{document}$i=L-1$\end{document} and τL−ℓ−1=1\begin{document}$\tau_{L-\ell-1}=1$\end{document} then sample t0∼Exp⁡(λL−ℓ−1)\begin{document}$t_0 \sim \operatorname{Exp}(\lambda_{L-\ell-1})$\end{document} and insert (L−ℓ−1,t0)\begin{document}$(L-\ell-1,t_0)$\end{document} into Ω.

If i=ℓ−1\begin{document}$i=\ell-1$\end{document}, then sample t0∼Exp⁡(α)\begin{document}$t_0 \sim \operatorname{Exp}(\alpha)$\end{document} and insert (−1,t0)\begin{document}$(-1,t_0)$\end{document} into Ω.

If i>ℓ−1\begin{document}$i\gt\ell-1$\end{document} and τi−ℓ=1\begin{document}$\tau_{i-\ell}=1$\end{document} , then sample t0∼Exp⁡(λi−ℓ)\begin{document}$t_0 \sim \operatorname{Exp}(\lambda_{i-\ell})$\end{document} and insert (i−ℓ,t0)\begin{document}$(i-\ell,t_0)$\end{document} into Ω.

If i<L−ℓ\begin{document}$i \lt L-\ell$\end{document} and τi+ℓ+1=0\begin{document}$\tau_{i+\ell+1}=0$\end{document} or if L−ℓ≤i≤L−2\begin{document}$L-\ell \leq i \leq L-2$\end{document} , then sample t0∼Exp⁡(λi+1)\begin{document}$t_0 \sim \operatorname{Exp}(\lambda_{i+1})$\end{document} and insert (i+1,t0)\begin{document}$(i+1,t_0)$\end{document} into Ω.

sec.p: The algorithm easily generalizes to a two-state telegraph model for initiation: the state of the system is now described by τ\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\tau}$\end{document} and G∈{0,1}\begin{document}$G\in\{0,1\}$\end{document}, with G=0\begin{document}$G=0$\end{document} if the transcript is inactive and G=1\begin{document}$G=1$\end{document} if the transcript is active. In the inactive state, initiation is not possible, while in the active state, initiation is an exponential process with rate α. In practice, we add the reaction ωk[0]=−2\begin{document}$\omega_{k}[0]=-2$\end{document} that corresponds to a change in transcript activity, G→¬G\begin{document}$G\rightarrow\neg G$\end{document}. The reaction time is drawn from an exponential distributionωk[1]∼{Exp⁡(k1)if G=0Exp⁡(k0)if G=1.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \omega_{k}[1] \sim \begin{cases}\operatorname{Exp}(k_{1})&\text{if }G=0 \\ \operatorname{Exp}(k_{0})&\text{if }G=1. \\\end{cases}$$\end{document}

We first simulate the model for 103 iterations of burn-in; for each trace, we record the state of the system every dt=20\begin{document}$dt=20$\end{document} s for a total duration of 90 min. The final state is used as a starting point to simulate the dynamics for 103 iterations before recording the following trace. For the run-off, we remove the initiation events possibly stored in Ω\begin{document}$\Omega$\end{document} after the initial burn-in and before simulating the first trace with α=0\begin{document}$\alpha=0$\end{document}. The last state of burn-in is then used as a starting point to simulate the dynamics for 103 more iterations before recording the following run-off.

To obtain the translation signal, we compute the number of epitopes n(t)\begin{document}$n(t)$\end{document} synthesized at each time point t\begin{document}$t$\end{document} by all the translating ribosomes. This is given by the scalar product(42)n(t)=w⋅τ(t)\begin{document}$$\displaystyle n(t) = \boldsymbol{w}\cdot \boldsymbol{\tau}(t)$$\end{document}

where w∈NL\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{w}\in\mathbb{N}^{L}$\end{document}, with L\begin{document}$L$\end{document} the transcript size, containing the cumulative number of epitopes along the transcript, and 𝝉∈{0,1}L\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\tau}\in\{0,1\}^{L}$\end{document} is the time-dependent occupation vector, with(43)τi={1if site i is occupied0if site i is free.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \tau_{i} = \begin{cases}1 &\text{if site }i\text{ is occupied} \\ 0 &\text{if site }i\text{ is free}.\end{cases}$$\end{document}

The total intensity is given by IS(t)=iMP24n(t)\begin{document}$I_{\mathrm{S}}(t)=\frac{i_{\mathrm{MP}}}{24}n(t)$\end{document}, where iMP\begin{document}$i_{\mathrm{MP}}$\end{document} is the intensity of one mature protein and the suffix S\begin{document}$\mathrm{S}$\end{document} stands for ‘simulated’.

We add an offset b0\begin{document}$b_{0}$\end{document} to the intensity and we apply a log-normal noise to obtain the final simulated, noisy signal yS(t)\begin{document}$y_{\mathrm{S}}(t)$\end{document}:(44)P(yS(t)|μS(t),s0)=1yS(t)s02πexp⁡{−(ln⁡yS(t)−ln⁡μS(t))22s02}\begin{document}$$\displaystyle P(y_{\mathrm{S}}(t) \,|\, \mu_{\mathrm{S}}(t), s_{0}) = \frac{1}{y_{\mathrm{S}}(t) s_{0} \sqrt{2\pi}}\exp{\left\{ -\frac{(\ln{y_{\mathrm{S}}(t)}-\ln{\mu_{\mathrm{S}}(t)})^{2}}{2s_{0}^{2}}\right\} }$$\end{document}

where μS(t)=b0+IS(t)\begin{document}$\mu_{\mathrm{S}}(t)=b_{0}+I_{\mathrm{S}}(t)$\end{document} and s0\begin{document}$s_{0}$\end{document} is the measurement noise, as described at the end of Bayesian modeling of run-off traces.

The duration of each trace is drawn from an exponential distribution with mean 10 min to mimic the tracking duration of one mRNA molecule in experiments. Simulated run-off traces are longer than 5 min (as in experiments, see Spot tracking and intensity quantification) and start at t=0\begin{document}$t=0$\end{document}, when the run-off starts (approximately 60 s after HT addition, see Preprocessing and data filtering).

sec.title: Data availability
sec.p: Our computational models for analysis of SunTag traces are available on Github (https://github.com/naef-lab/suntag-analysis copy archived at Lamberti, 2026). SunTag run-off and steady-state raw images are available on Zenodo (https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.17669331).

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: We would like to thank the EPFL’s Bioimaging and Optics Facility (BIOP) and Scientific IT & Application Support (SCITAS) for their assistance, as well as Eva Poch for her help with the Western blot experiments. We also appreciate the insightful discussions with Tobias Hochstoeger regarding the SunTag system. This research was supported by the Swiss National Science Foundation (SNSF) Sinergia grant #205884 to FN and JC, the Swiss Cancer Research grant #KFS-5412-08-2021-R to FN,and the École Polytechnique Fédérale de Lausanne.
